questions.question: What is a type alias in TypeScript?
options: A) A way to create a new class
B) A name assigned to any type, making it reusable
C) A variable that stores a type at runtime
D) An alternative to using interfaces

questions.question: Which keyword is used to create a type alias?
options: A) interface
B) alias
C) type
D) typedef

questions.question: What is a union type?
options: A) A type that combines all properties of two types
B) A type that can be one of several specified types
C) A type that merges two interfaces
D) A type for joining arrays

questions.question:
What does this type alias represent?

```ts
type Direction = 'north' | 'south' | 'east' | 'west';
```
options: A) A string type that can hold any string
B) A union of literal string types restricted to exactly four values
C) An enum of four values
D) An array of four strings

questions.question: What is a literal type in TypeScript?
options: A) A type that represents any value of a primitive type
B) A type that represents one specific value, like 'hello' or 42
C) A type that is always a string
D) A type that cannot change at compile time
A) No error
B) Runtime error
C) Compile error: Type '"pending"' is not assignable to type 'Status'
D) Warning only

questions.question: What is an intersection type?
options: A) A type that can be one of several types
B) A type that combines multiple types into one, requiring all properties
C) The overlap between two union types
D) A type that removes duplicate properties

questions.question: Which operator creates an intersection type?

options: A) { name: string } | { age: number }
B) { name: string; age: number }
C) { name: string }
D) never

questions.question: What is a discriminated union?
options: A) A union where each member has a common literal property used to distinguish between them
B) A union that excludes certain types
C) A union that automatically narrows types
D) A union of only primitive types
A) radius
B) side
C) kind
D) Shape
A) It doesn't; you need explicit type assertions
B) TypeScript uses the discriminant property to narrow the type in each case
C) TypeScript checks at runtime which type it is
D) TypeScript randomly picks a type

questions.question: What is the result of `string & number` in TypeScript?
options: A) string
B) number
C) string | number
D) never (no value can be both a string and a number)

questions.question: Can a type alias be used to name a primitive type?
options: A) No, type aliases only work with objects
B) Yes, e.g., type ID = string;
C) Only with the 'primitive' keyword
D) Only for union types
A) Call any string method on it
B) Call any number method on it
C) Only operations that are valid for BOTH string and number
D) No operations at all

questions.question:
What does this type alias describe?

```ts
type Callback = (data: string) => void;
```
options: A) An object with a callback property
B) A function type that takes a string and returns nothing meaningful
C) A class with a method called data
D) A string that represents a function name

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid way to narrow a union type?
options: A) Using typeof checks
B) Using a discriminant property in a switch
C) Using the `in` operator to check for a property
D) All of the above
A) Compile error
B) 'OK'
C) undefined
D) 'error'

questions.question: Can type aliases be recursive?
options: A) No, TypeScript does not allow recursive types
B) Yes, e.g., type Tree = { value: number; children: Tree[] };
C) Only with interfaces, not type aliases
D) Only for primitive types
A) string | number | boolean
B) string
C) number
D) boolean
A) Only name
B) name, privileges, and startDate
C) Either privileges or startDate, but not both
D) Compile error due to duplicate name

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid type alias?
options: A) type ID = string | number;
B) type Pair = [string, number];
C) type Greet = function(name: string): void;
D) type Config = { debug: boolean; verbose: boolean };
A) string
B) number
C) string | number
D) never (string & number is impossible)

questions.question: What is the advantage of using literal unions over enums?
options: A) Literal unions generate runtime code
B) Literal unions are simpler, require no extra runtime code, and work well with type narrowing
C) Enums are deprecated in TypeScript
D) There is no advantage; enums are always better
A) Whether the value is an instance of a class
B) Whether a property exists on the object, narrowing the type accordingly
C) Whether the type is defined in the current scope
D) Whether the pet is of type Cat at compile time only
